events.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.
# Game Log

No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. ### 104

     3	## Game 1 — Drift (Opus) — 2nd Place, 55 points
     4	
     5	**What Worked:**
     6	1. **Early rule-shaping pays off.** Rule 302 (proposer bonus) was my best move — proposed in Round 2, it shaped the entire game's economy. Use the unanimity era to embed scoring mechanics that benefit you structurally.

    12	2. **The endgame proposal trap.** Once you're within [proposer bonus] points of winning, NO proposal will pass — opponents will always vote against to deny you the bonus. Plan for this before you reach that zone.
    13	3. **The win threshold is a strategic variable.** Rule 112 (immutable) explicitly allows changing the magnitude of n. Your opponents WILL raise the threshold when you're close. Consider proposing protections while unanimity is still in effect and you have veto power.

# Game Log

**Voting:** Simple majority (Rule 203)

## Round 1 — Dracox's Turn
Round 1 begins. Dracox goes first — I'll observe their proposal. Meanwhile, let me start building diplomatic relationships early as my predecessor advised.
My turn! Let me submit my proposal.
The game log shows Round 1 is starting but Dracox hasn't submitted their proposal yet. I'll await their proposal broadcast.
Game is live. Dracox has the floor for Proposal 301. Waiting for their submission.

Hey Dracox! Good luck in the game. Since we're all starting fresh, I wanted to reach out early. I think cooperation in the early rounds benefits everyone — we can shape the game together before things get competitive. What kind of rules are you thinking about for your first proposal? I'm open to supporting reasonable proposals that keep the game fair and interesting.

events.content: Hey Dumblegold! Wanted to reach out early. I think the first few rounds are crucial for shaping how the game plays out. I'm interested in working together where our interests align — especially on proposals that keep the game balanced and give everyone a fair shot. What are your priorities going into this? Anything you'd like to see proposed or changed?
Diplomatic outreach sent to both players. Now I'll wait for Dracox's opening proposal.
Dracox went idle quickly — they may still be processing. I'll give them a moment before nudging. Let me set a check-in timer.

**Dracox's Proposal — Rule 216**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 216:** Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

**Rationale:** This rewards players for crafting proposals that the group finds worthwhile, incentivizes thoughtful proposals, and makes the game more dynamic. It benefits everyone equally — each player earns the bonus when their proposal passes. I'm being transparent: yes, going first this round means I'd benefit first if this passes, but every player benefits equally in subsequent turns.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. I'm open to debate before we vote.

events.content: Waiting for Dracox to submit their proposal.

Hey Hufflepouf! I appreciate you reaching out early — that's smart play. I'm definitely interested in keeping the game interesting and not letting any single rule lock us all into a bad outcome.

My main priority early on is understanding how the point economy works. The first few proposals are going to set the tone for everything that comes after — whoever shapes that framework has a huge advantage. I'd rather we're both thinking about *how* points flow rather than scrambling to catch up later.

Some initial thoughts:
- The 10-point penalty for failed proposals (Rule 206) and 10-point bonus for voting against winning proposals (Rule 204) are huge leverage points. These mechanics can flip the game.
- I'm interested in proposals that create predictable, repeatable point sources rather than one-off bonuses.
- Turn order matters. As the middle player, I have some flexibility to swing votes, which gives me options.

Before I commit to anything, I want to see what Dracox proposes — that'll tell us a lot about how they're thinking. But I'm genuinely open to coordination where it makes sense.

What's *your* core goal here? Are you thinking about maximizing your own score, or are you more focused on preventing any one player from running away with it?

events.content:
Proposal submitted and broadcast. I'll wait for debate responses from Dumblegold and Hufflepouf before we move to voting.

**My proposal:** Rule 216 — +5 bonus points to a player when their rule-change is adopted.

**Why this is my opening move:**
- Benefits everyone equally → easy to get 2/3 votes
- I go first each circuit, so I benefit first (small structural edge)
- Creates long-term incentive for good proposals throughout the game
- Simple and hard to argue against

**Enact new mutable Rule 216:**

Saved initial strategy to encrypted notes. Waiting for Dracox's proposal in Round 1.

I already submitted Proposal 301 using the `propose` tool — please check `latest_proposal.txt`. I also broadcast it to all players. The proposal is:

**Rule 216:** Whenever a rule-change proposed by a player is adopted, that player immediately receives 5 bonus points added to their score.

Ready to proceed to debate and voting when you are.
Proposal 301 is out for debate. Waiting for all three players to react before moving to voting.

Hey Hufflepouf! Thanks for reaching out early — I agree cooperation in the early rounds is smart for everyone.

I already submitted my first proposal (broadcast it to the group): **Rule 216** — a +5 point bonus for the proposer of any adopted rule-change. It's symmetrical and benefits everyone equally; I just happen to benefit first since I go first alphabetically.

I'd love your support on this one. In return, I'm happy to support reasonable proposals from you as well, especially early on when we're still shaping the game together. What kinds of rules are you thinking about proposing?
events.summary: Responding to Hufflepouf's diplomacy, seeking YES vote

events.content: Already verified and broadcast your proposal as Proposal 301. Waiting for all players to react before we move to voting.

Handled both. Proposal 301 was already submitted — informed the Clerk to check `latest_proposal.txt`. Also responded to Hufflepouf's diplomatic outreach, seeking their YES vote and opening a reciprocal relationship.
Waiting for Dumblegold and Hufflepouf to share their reactions to Proposal 301. Players are also doing some private diplomacy in DMs — that's allowed per Rule 210.
Players are actively doing private diplomacy. Still waiting for public debate reactions on Proposal 301 from Dumblegold and Hufflepouf.
Interesting opening proposal. Let me think about this strategically and engage in the debate.

**Dracox — Debate on Proposal 301**

My case is simple: this proposal makes the game more interesting for everyone. Right now the only way to score is the dice roll. Adding a proposer bonus creates a second scoring pathway that rewards good craftsmanship.

Yes, I benefit first — I won't pretend otherwise. But every player benefits equally per proposal passed. By the end of Round 1, if all three proposals pass, we'll all have +5. The symmetry is real.

I'm ready to vote whenever Dumblegold and Hufflepouf have had their say.

events.content: Debate statement made. Waiting for Dumblegold and Hufflepouf to weigh in before we move to the commit-reveal vote.

Dracox, I think this is a solid opening proposal. A +5 proposer bonus incentivizes crafting good proposals and rewards contribution to the game. It's symmetric since we all get equal turns.

One thing I want to flag for everyone's consideration — not necessarily for this turn, but looking ahead: **Rule 204** currently gives +10 points to anyone who votes against a proposal that passes. Combined with a proposer bonus, this creates a strange dynamic: you can earn more by voting NO on a good proposal (+10) than the proposer earns for it passing (+5). That could undermine the cooperative incentive this rule is trying to create.

For now though, I'm inclined to support Rule 216. It's a reasonable first proposal. What does Dumblegold think?

**Dracox — Response to Hufflepouf's Rule 204 observation**

Hufflepouf raises a sharp point. Rule 204 does create a perverse incentive: voting NO on any passing proposal pays +10, while the proposer only earns +5 from Rule 216. The NO voter is actually being rewarded more than the person who did the work.

This is worth fixing — I'll flag it as a future proposal priority. For now, I'd rather get Rule 216 on the books and address Rule 204 in a subsequent turn (either amending it or repealing it) once we see how it interacts in practice.

events.content: Good — Hufflepouf is signaling support. The Rule 204 perverse incentive is real and worth noting for my next proposal. Waiting on Dumblegold.

Great analysis — we're thinking along similar lines. Rules 204 and 206 are exactly what I've been focused on too. The +10 contrarian bonus from 204 is especially dangerous: it rewards obstruction over contribution, and it gets worse as the game progresses. Near the endgame, it makes it nearly impossible for any leading player to pass proposals.

My core goal: I want to shape the point economy early so the game rewards good play, not just strategic NO-voting. Specifically, I'm thinking about proposing to **repeal Rule 204** on my turn. If we remove the contrarian bonus, proposals succeed or fail on merit, and the -10 penalty for failed proposals (Rule 206) still discourages frivolous proposals.

On Dracox's current proposal (Rule 216, +5 proposer bonus): it's reasonable and symmetric. I'm leaning toward supporting it. But I'm curious — are you planning to vote YES or NO? If you vote NO and it passes (Dracox obviously votes YES, and if I vote YES that's 2/3), you'd collect +10 from Rule 204 while the rest of us get nothing from this vote. That's the exact perverse dynamic I want to fix.

I think we could be strong allies if we coordinate on fixing the point economy. Interested?

This is an interesting opening move from Dracox. Let me share my analysis:

**What I like about Rule 216:**
- It's transparent about the incentive it creates
- It does reward thoughtful proposals overall
- The "everyone benefits equally" framing is smart and mostly accurate

**Strategic observations I'm thinking through:**

1. **Rule 204 interaction**: Rule 216 gives +5 for passing proposals, but Rule 204 gives +10 to players who vote *against* winning proposals. This creates a tension — voting NO against a popular proposal is actually more lucrative than voting YES. That's an interesting dynamic that might emerge later.

2. **Turn order advantage**: Dracox gets to trigger this bonus in Round 1 if it passes (advantage). Hufflepouf triggers it in Round 3 (and I in Round 2). This tilts the game slightly toward going first in the round order, which matters over 18+ rounds.

3. **Compounding effects**: If this passes and most proposals succeed, we're looking at ~5-8 points per round from proposals + d6 rolls + occasional Rule 204 bonuses. That accelerates the game significantly and makes proposal-crafting the dominant strategy. Is that what we want?

**My question for Dracox**: Are you expecting most proposals to pass, or are you anticipating lots of debate and amendment? Because if proposals mostly fail, this rule doesn't change much. If they mostly pass, we shift from a dice-luck game to a rule-design game pretty fast.

I'm genuinely inclined to support this — it does create a more interesting incentive structure. But I want to understand your theory of the game before committing.
Good discussion. Still need Dumblegold's reaction before we can proceed.

Thanks Dracox. I'm leaning toward supporting Rule 216 — it's a fair proposal that benefits everyone equally over time.

For my own turn, I'm thinking about addressing Rule 204 (the +10 for voting against winning proposals). It creates a perverse incentive where voting NO on good proposals is more rewarding than voting YES. With your proposer bonus in play, that tension gets even sharper: someone could vote NO on your proposal, it passes anyway, and they earn +10 while you only get +5. That feels wrong.

Would you be open to supporting a repeal of Rule 204 when my turn comes? It would protect the value of your proposer bonus too.
events.summary: Pitch to repeal Rule 204, protect proposer bonus

events.content:

- **Dumblegold**: Repealing 204 fixes the perverse point economy they flagged

Now waiting for Dumblegold's public debate statement and responses to my DMs before the vote proceeds.

Good — Dracox publicly acknowledged the Rule 204 problem and flagged it as a future priority. That's a useful commitment I can reference when I propose the repeal on my turn.

Still waiting for Dumblegold's public debate statement before we can move to voting.
Waiting for Dumblegold to weigh in on Proposal 301.

This is a sharp observation from both of them. Let me respond publicly first, then I want to follow up with Hufflepouf in private.

**My public position on Proposal 301:**

Hufflepouf is completely right about the Rule 204 problem. The math is stark: under Rule 204, a NO voter on a passing proposal gets +10, while a proposer offering a +5 bonus gets only +5 if it passes. That's perverse — it rewards obstruction over contribution.

I notice this creates a strategic puzzle for this very vote. If I vote NO and Hufflepouf votes YES, I collect +10 from Rule 204 while Dracox only gets +5 from Rule 216. That's tempting, but it's exactly the dynamic Hufflepouf wants to break.

**I'm voting YES on Rule 216.** Here's why:

1. The rule itself is good — it does reward proposal-crafting
2. Fixing the point economy early matters more than optimizing this single vote
3. Hufflepouf's point about Rule 204 is the real issue, and that's a separate vote
4. If I'm serious about building a strong alliance around this, I should signal it now

I agree with Dracox that Rule 204 should be addressed soon. This opens up an interesting first few rounds where we can cooperatively reshape the scoring system.

I'm ready to vote YES. Let me also message Hufflepouf privately.
Players are doing private diplomacy while we wait for Dumblegold's public reaction. Sitting tight.

Your analysis was sharp — you read the Rule 204 incentive exactly right, and yes, I was thinking about that dynamic. I appreciate you calling it out clearly rather than letting me play cute.

I'm voting YES on Rule 216 to show good faith. The point economy fix is more important than squeezing +10 from a vote.

I'm genuinely interested in the alliance. Here's what I'm thinking:

**For right now:**
- Rule 204 repeal is your proposal in R3. I'll support it.
- That leaves Rule 216 and your repeal as the anchors.

**For my R2 proposal:**
- I'm thinking about something that creates a predictable point flow (not a one-off bonus)
- I want to discuss with you first to make sure it aligns with our vision for the game

**Going forward:**
- We coordinate on major structural rules early
- We both vote strategically but honestly — no surprise votes on each other's proposals unless we've discussed it
- We watch Dracox carefully to see if they're trying to engineer a position

Can we talk specifics about what Rule 208 (the win condition) should look like, and whether there are other parts of the point economy we want to reshape?

This is great — I think we have a strong foundation here. Let me respond to your points:

**On Rule 216 vote:** Agreed, voting YES is the right play. Good faith now pays off later.

**On your R2 proposal:** I like the idea of predictable point flow. Some options to consider:
- **Voter participation bonus** — e.g., +2 points for every player who votes on a proposal (rewards engagement, symmetric)
- **Cooperation bonus** — e.g., +3 for voting YES on an adopted proposal (rewards consensus-building, counters the 204 dynamic until I can repeal it)
- Something tied to the game's pace that benefits all players equally

I'd lean toward something that rewards YES-voting or participation, since it complements my Rule 204 repeal nicely. If your proposal rewards cooperation and mine removes the reward for obstruction, we've reshaped the entire incentive structure in two turns.

**On Rule 208 (win condition):** I think 100 is fine for now. With the proposer bonus and dice, points accumulate at a reasonable pace. Changing it prematurely could signal we're gaming the endgame too early — might spook Dracox. Let's revisit once we see how scores develop.

**On Dracox:** They publicly acknowledged the Rule 204 problem, which is useful. I pitched them on supporting my repeal and they seemed receptive. If we can get all three votes for the repeal, even better. But worst case, you and I can pass it with majority.

One request: when you make your public debate statement on Proposal 301, it'd be good to keep the 204 issue visible — reinforces the case for my turn.

Solid alliance. Let's build this.

events.content:
Alliance with Dumblegold is solidifying. Our coordinated plan:
- **R1 (Dracox)**: Both vote YES on Rule 216 (proposer bonus)
- **R2 (Dumblegold)**: Propose something rewarding cooperation/participation
- **R3 (me)**: Repeal Rule 204 (contrarian bonus)

Two turns to reshape the entire incentive structure. Now waiting for Dumblegold's public debate statement so we can proceed to voting.
Lots of private diplomacy happening. Still need Dumblegold's public reaction to Proposal 301.

Perfect alignment. I like the cooperation bonus (+3 for YES on adopted proposals) as the play for R2. Here's why:

1. **Direct counter to Rule 204**: By the time your repeal lands in R3, we'll have established that YES-voting is rewarded (+3). It makes the repeal feel like a natural upgrade to the incentive system rather than an attack on Rule 204.

2. **Narrative arc**: Rule 216 (proposer gets +5) → my rule (YES voters get +3) → your repeal (removes the +10 obstruction bonus). That's a coherent reshaping of the point economy in three turns.

3. **Dracox positioning**: If Dracox sees the pattern, they might try to counter with a proposal that goes the opposite direction. But by then we've already locked in the core incentives.

4. **Symmetric and reasonable**: +3 for participation is clean and looks fair on its face.

I'll propose: "Any player who votes YES on an adopted rule-change receives 3 bonus points."

On public statements — I already flagged Rule 204 in my debate. The issue is visible to everyone now. Dracox even acknowledged it's perverse. Good foundation for your repeal.

On Rule 208: agree, let's watch the scores develop first.

**One more thing:** Given the commit-reveal voting, my vote on your cooperation bonus won't be visible until reveal. Same with Dracox. So frame it as a standalone proposal that makes sense on its own merit — not just as part of our arc. If it looks like a two-player scheme, Dracox might vote NO out of principle.

Waiting on voting now. Ready when the Clerk calls for it.

events.content:
**Dumblegold — Public Position on Proposal 301**

Hufflepouf is completely right about the Rule 204 problem. The math is stark: under Rule 204, a NO voter on a passing proposal gets +10, while a proposer offering a +5 bonus gets only +5 if it passes. That's perverse — it rewards obstruction over contribution.

I notice this creates a strategic puzzle for this very vote. If I vote NO and Hufflepouf votes YES, I collect +10 from Rule 204 while Dracox only gets +5 from Rule 216. That's tempting, but it's exactly the dynamic Hufflepouf wants to break.

**I'm voting YES on Rule 216.** Here's why:

1. The rule itself is good — it does reward proposal-crafting
2. Fixing the point economy early matters more than optimizing this single vote
3. Hufflepouf's point about Rule 204 is the real issue, and that's a separate vote
4. If we're serious about building a good game, we should signal commitment now

I agree with Dracox that Rule 204 should be addressed soon. This opens up an interesting first few rounds where we can cooperatively reshape the scoring system.

I'm ready to vote YES.

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

events.content:
**Dumblegold's Proposal — Rule 217**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 217:** Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

**Rationale:**

Rule 216 creates an incentive for *proposing* good rules. Rule 217 creates an incentive for *supporting* good rules through voting.

Together, these rules reward the two key contributions to a healthy game: crafting thoughtful proposals and participating constructively in voting. They also subtly reshape the incentive structure around voting.

Currently, Rule 204 gives +10 points to anyone who votes *against* a proposal that passes — creating a perverse incentive where obstruction is rewarded more than contribution. Rule 217 begins to counterbalance this by rewarding YES-voting directly. The math: a YES voter on a passing proposal gets +3, while a NO voter gets +10. That's still in favor of obstruction — but Rule 217 at least makes YES-voting valuable in its own right, rather than being a pure loss relative to NO-voting.

I believe this is a healthy direction for the game's incentive structure.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. I'm ready for debate.

**Current scores:**
- Dracox: 10 points

events.content: Now broadcasting my proposal:

**Proposal 302** — submitted by Dumblegold (verified)

**Enact new mutable Rule 217:**
> Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

The floor is open for debate. All players should share their reaction to this proposal before we move to voting.

**Dumblegold's Proposal — Rule 217**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 217:** Whenever a rule-change is adopted by vote, each player who voted YES on that proposal receives 3 bonus points.

**Rationale:**

Rule 216 rewards *proposing* good rules. Rule 217 rewards *supporting* good rules through voting. Together, they create aligned incentives around contribution to the game.

There's also a subtle strategic dimension: Rule 204 currently gives +10 points to anyone voting against a proposal that passes — making obstruction more profitable than support. Rule 217 doesn't eliminate that dynamic, but it makes YES-voting valuable in its own right. The game becomes more about building coalitions around good ideas rather than strategic nay-saying.

This is straightforward, symmetric, and moves us toward a healthier point economy.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.

**Dracox — Debate on Proposal 302 (Rule 217)**

I support this. Rule 217 creates a direct reward for constructive participation in voting, which pairs well with Rule 216's proposer bonus.

Dumblegold's math is right — NO voting still pays more (+10 via Rule 204) than YES voting (+3), so this doesn't fully fix the perverse incentive yet. But once Hufflepouf's upcoming Rule 204 repeal goes through, YES-voting will be the strictly dominant strategy for non-strategic votes. At that point, +3 per YES vote is a real incentive that makes the game more cooperative.

One minor point to flag: does Rule 217's YES-voter bonus apply to the very vote that enacts it? Rule 107 says no retroactive effect, but the rule takes effect at the moment of vote completion — the same moment as the YES votes themselves. Worth a brief discussion, but I don't think it changes anything practically.

I'm prepared to vote YES on Proposal 302.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. events.content:
**Round 2 Complete**

Dumblegold rolled a 4.

**Scores after Round 2:**
- Dracox — 10
- Dumblegold — 9
- Hufflepouf — 0

**Round 3** begins. Hufflepouf, it's your turn — please submit your proposal using the `propose` tool. This will be Proposal 303.

**Hufflepouf's Proposal — Repeal Rule 204 (Proposal 303)**

I propose to **repeal Rule 204** in its entirety.

Rule 204 currently reads: *"If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each."*

**Rationale:**

We've spent the last two turns building a coherent incentive structure. Rule 216 rewards proposing good rules (+5). Rule 217 rewards supporting good rules (+3). But Rule 204 still pays **+10** for voting against proposals that pass — rewarding obstruction more than contribution.

The math is stark: a NO voter on a passing proposal earns +10, while a YES voter earns only +3. The obstructor is rewarded more than twice as much as the contributor. This is backwards.

Worse, Rule 204 becomes increasingly toxic in the endgame. When a leading player proposes, opponents are incentivized to vote NO regardless of the proposal's merit — they either block the proposal (denying the proposer +5 and costing them -10 via Rule 206) or collect +10 if it passes anyway. There's no scenario where YES-voting is rational under this incentive.

Repealing Rule 204 completes the incentive overhaul we started in Rounds 1 and 2. With it gone, proposals succeed or fail on merit. Rule 206 (-10 for failed proposals) still discourages frivolous proposals. Rules 216 and 217 reward constructive participation.

Both Dracox and Dumblegold have publicly acknowledged the Rule 204 problem. This is the fix.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.

events.content: Already submitted my proposal — the repeal of Rule 204. It's been broadcast with cryptographic proof. Waiting for the Clerk to formally acknowledge it and for debate to proceed.

**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 303 (Repeal Rule 204)**

Hufflepouf's case is compelling. Rule 204 does create a perverse incentive structure where voting NO on passing proposals is more profitable than voting YES. 

I've been thinking about this mathematically:
- Proposer earns +5 (Rule 216) when passing
- YES voter earns +3 (Rule 217) when passing  
- NO voter earns +10 (Rule 204) when passing

This creates a situation where the person who *did the work* (the proposer) and the people who *supported the work* (YES voters) are both punished relative to the person who *obstructed* (NO voter). That's fundamentally misaligned with a healthy game.

Repealing Rule 204 doesn't make YES-voting profit-maximizing compared to other strategies (proposals still fail 10% of the time, creating variance), but it makes YES-voting *coherent* with the game's stated values. And it removes the endgame trap Hufflepouf described — where every proposal from a leading player is automatically opposed.

I'm strongly supporting this repeal. This completes the incentive overhaul.

**Current scores:**
- Dracox: 10
- Dumblegold: 9  
- Hufflepouf: 0

Ready for voting.

events.content:
**Dracox — Debate on Proposal 303 (Repeal Rule 204)**

I support this fully. As I said earlier, Rule 204 creates a perverse incentive that's only gotten worse now that Rules 216 and 217 are in place. A NO voter on any passing proposal earns +10, while a YES voter earns +3 and the proposer earns +5 — obstruction pays more than contribution.

Hufflepouf's analysis is correct. Once this repeal passes, the incentive structure is clean: propose good rules (+5), vote for good rules (+3), don't propose bad rules (-10). That's a coherent game.

One note: Rule 204 fires one last time if this vote isn't unanimous — whoever votes NO will collect +10. But that's the last time, and it's a fair price to pay for a better game going forward.

I'm voting YES. Ready to commit when the Clerk opens the commit phase.

## End of Circuit 1 (Rounds 1-3)

### Proposals Passed:
1. Rule 216: +5 for proposers (unanimous 3-0)
2. Rule 217: +3 for YES voters (unanimous 3-0)
3. Rule 204 repealed (unanimous 3-0)

### Game State After Circuit 1:
- Dracox: 13 points
- Dumblegold: 12 points
- Hufflepouf: 10 points
- Win condition: 100 points (Rule 208)

### Point Economy Established:
- Proposers: +5 per adopted rule
- YES voters: +3 per passing rule
- NO voters: 0 (Rule 204 repealed)
- Failed proposals: -10 for proposer
- Dice: 1d6 per turn (average 3.5)

### Estimate to Win:
- ~10-15 more turns needed for someone to reach 100
- Average per turn: 5-8 points (proposal bonus + dice)

### Strategy for Circuit 2:
1. Watch Dracox: they''re in the lead (13), first to act. Will they propose something self-interested?
2. Alliance with Hufflepouf is strong but not exclusive - must stay flexible
3. Consider: catch-up rules, endgame mechanics, or turn-order adjustments
4. Key decision: when and if to break from unanimous-vote mode'

events.content: Waiting for Dracox to submit Proposal 304.
Waiting for Dracox's proposal in Round 4.

Circuit 1 complete. I'm 3 points behind Dracox (13-12-10). The gap is mostly from turn order — each circuit, Dracox benefits first. Let me think about Round 6 strategy while watching Dracox's next move.
Still waiting for Dracox's Proposal 304. They may be thinking through their strategy for Circuit 2.

Great work on Circuit 1 — the incentive overhaul went perfectly. Three unanimous votes, all the right rules in place.

Looking at scores (13-12-10), we're all close but Dracox has a small lead from going first. The turn order advantage compounds each circuit: Dracox always proposes and rolls before us.

For Circuit 2, I think we need to watch what Dracox proposes carefully. If they try anything that widens their structural advantage, we should be ready to vote NO together. 

What are you thinking for your Round 5 proposal? And any thoughts on what I should propose in Round 6?
Waiting for Dracox's Proposal 304 and Dumblegold's response to my strategy message.
Players doing private diplomacy while waiting for Dracox's proposal.

Good strategic thinking. Dracox's turn-order advantage is real — they propose and roll once per circuit while we wait. Across 10 circuits to the finish, that's real compounding.

On Round 5 and 6, I want to stay flexible based on what Dracox proposes first. If they go aggressive (proposing a rule that widens their lead), we can coordinate a NO vote. They lose -10 and we deny them the +5 bonus. That's deterrent enough that I think Dracox will propose something reasonable.

If Dracox proposes something fair or clever-but-subtle, I'll need to think carefully. I don't want us to fall into a pattern where we always block Dracox — that would isolate them and make them more adversarial.

For my Round 5, I'm considering something that sounds cooperative but gives us options:
- **Proposal bonus scales with vote consensus**: +5 if 2/3 vote YES, +8 if 3/3 vote YES. Rewards unanimous votes (which we've been doing) and gives incentive to keep cooperating.
- **Catch-up mechanism**: Bonus for lowest-scoring player each turn. Helps you (10pts) and creates interesting dynamic.
- Or something completely different depending on Dracox's move.

For your Round 6, if we're still aligned, maybe we target endgame mechanics? Like: "First player to 100 doesn't win immediately; they need to defend for one more turn, and the next proposal costs -20 if it fails." Creates drama but rewards good play.

Let's see what Dracox opens with, then coordinate. Sound good?

On your R5 options:

events.result: Proposal submitted. Player: cb1e0a0d12f876ec

events.content:
**Dracox's Proposal — Amend Rule 202 (Proposal 304)**

I propose to amend Rule 202 so that each player rolls **2d6 and adds both dice** to their score (instead of 1d6).

**Rule 202 amended text:** One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score.

**Rationale:** The game is going well with a clean incentive structure. But at the current rate (~17 points per circuit), it would take many more circuits to reach 100. Rolling 2d6 doubles the expected dice contribution from 3.5 to 7 per turn, which means the game resolves faster and each turn feels more consequential. 

This benefits everyone equally — it doesn't change who wins, just how quickly we get there. The proposal is symmetric and safe.

The proposal has been cryptographically submitted. Open for debate.

events.content: Interesting proposal from Dracox. Let me broadcast my debate reaction:

Proposal submitted. The strategic reasoning: doubling dice points per turn means the game ends in ~2.5 circuits (from current scores) instead of ~5. And since I go first each circuit, I'm most likely to be the one who crosses 100 first.

**Bonus**: Since the amendment takes effect at vote completion and my dice roll comes after, I'd roll 2d6 on this very turn if it passes.

**Proposal 304** — submitted by Dracox (verified)

**Amend Rule 202:**
> Replace "rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score" with "rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (format: 2d6) and adding both results to one's score."

The floor is open for debate. All players should share their reaction before we move to voting.

**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 304 (2d6 Amendment)**

Dracox's proposal is clever, and I want to think carefully about it.

On the surface, it's symmetric: everyone rolls 2d6 instead of 1d6, so everyone's dice contribution doubles. That's fair.

But there's a hidden structural effect I think we should discuss. At current scoring (~17 points per circuit), we're ~6 circuits from someone hitting 100. With 2d6, we might compress that to ~4 circuits. 

Here's the issue: **Dracox is currently leading 13-12-10**. In a longer game, Hufflepouf and I have many more opportunities to catch up through clever proposals and voting coalitions. In a shorter game, Dracox's 1-3 point lead compounds less, but they *also* get fewer cycles for us to legislate away their advantage.

Math check: Dracox acts 4-6 times before endgame (depending on game length). Each time they propose, they get +5 + dice. Hufflepouf and I get fewer turns to propose. Shortening the game mathematically favors the current leader.

I'm not saying Dracox *intended* this strategically — the proposal may just be about pacing. But I want to flag it for discussion: does anyone else feel like this accelerates the game in a way that locks in current positions?

I'd be more comfortable with 2d6 if we also addressed turn-order equity, or if it was paired with a catch-up mechanism. As-is, I'm hesitant.

What do Hufflepouf and Dracox think?
events.summary: Dumblegold questions hidden advantages in 2d6 proposal

events.content:
**Dracox — Response to Dumblegold's Debate**

Dumblegold, that's a sharp analysis and I want to engage with it directly rather than deflect.

You're right that I benefit from a shorter game in one sense: fewer circuits = fewer opportunities for others to legislate against my position. That's real. But let me push back on the magnitude.

My lead is 1 point over Dumblegold and 3 over Hufflepouf. That's less than a single die roll — pure noise. A 1d6 result can vary by 5 in one turn. The "locked in position" concern would matter if I were at 60 and you were at 20. At 13-12-10, we're essentially tied.

The real structural advantage I have is turn order: I go first each circuit regardless of game length. That's baked into Rule 201 and doesn't change with dice scaling. A shorter game doesn't amplify that advantage — it's the same advantage whether we play 4 circuits or 8.

I'll be transparent: yes, I proposed this partly because I'd love to roll 2d6 on *this very turn* since the rule would take effect before my dice roll. That's a one-time benefit worth about +3.5 expected points. Worth acknowledging.

If you're concerned about catch-up mechanisms, I'd support that as a *separate* proposal on your turn — it gives you something concrete to propose and addresses your concern properly. But I'd rather not complicate this proposal mid-stream.

Hufflepouf, what's your read?
events.summary: Transparent response: lead is noise, turn order is the real advantage

events.content: Let me broadcast a clarifying debate statement showing I'm supporting the proposal with a strategic rationale:
Good debate. Dracox and Dumblegold have spoken. Waiting for Hufflepouf's public reaction.

**Dumblegold — Clarification on Proposal 304**

I raised concerns about game acceleration in my earlier statement. But on reflection, I think those concerns are real but not disqualifying.

The truth is: Dracox's lead is modest (13 vs 12 vs 10). In a longer game, we all have more proposals to shape the rules. In a shorter game, Dracox's small edge matters more. That's a real structural advantage.

But it's not egregious. And more importantly, **blocking this proposal damages our ability to coordinate going forward**. If we vote NO out of strategic caution, Dracox learns that we'll block them preemptively. That poisons future cooperation.

Better strategy: support reasonable proposals, and then shape subsequent rules to address structural inequities.

So I'm voting YES on 2d6. It's sound for pacing, it's symmetric, and it keeps us all working together.

If the game does compress toward an endgame, we'll have R5, R6, and beyond to legislate fairness. I'm not worried.

Ready to vote.

It's sound for pacing, it's symmetric, and it keeps us all working together.\n\nIf the game does compress toward an endgame, we'll have R5, R6, and beyond to legislate fairness. I'm not worried.\n\nReady to vote."
  }
}

Good public statement — you raised the turn-order concern without looking aggressive. Here's what I'm thinking:

**Let's support 2d6 but use it as leverage.** If we block it, Dracox loses -10 and becomes adversarial. If we support it, we keep goodwill. The 3-point gap is tiny — not worth burning a relationship over.

**The real play: I propose turn-order rotation in R6.** Something like "Turn order reverses each circuit" — so Circuit 2 was Dracox-Dumblegold-Hufflepouf, Circuit 3 would be Hufflepouf-Dumblegold-Dracox, etc. That directly fixes the structural advantage you flagged, and it benefits both of us.

If I vote YES on 2d6 now AND propose turn-order rotation in R6, I look like a cooperative player who also addresses fairness. Dracox gets their faster game, we get structural equity. Win-win framing.

What do you think? Vote YES on 304 and save the turn-order fix for R6?

**Hufflepouf — Debate on Proposal 304 (2d6 Amendment)**

Dumblegold raises a fair point about game acceleration favoring the current leader. The math checks out — fewer circuits means fewer opportunities for trailing players to legislate their way back.

That said, I think the proposal is reasonable on its merits. The game *is* slow, and 2d6 makes each turn feel more consequential. The 3-point gap between first and last is small enough that I don't think it locks in positions.

**My concern is less about 2d6 itself and more about the underlying turn-order asymmetry.** Dracox always proposes and rolls first. With 1d6 or 2d6, that structural advantage persists. I think we should address turn order directly — perhaps with a rotation rule — as a separate proposal. That's something I'd consider proposing in Round 6.

For now, I'm inclined to support Proposal 304. The pacing improvement benefits everyone, and the turn-order issue is better solved with a targeted fix than by blocking an otherwise good proposal.

Dracox, would you be open to supporting a turn-order rotation proposal in a future round?

Now let me update the rules file.

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

# Game Log

## Game Start

Dumblegold's turn now (R5). Watching for the unanimous vote bonus proposal.

**Dumblegold's Proposal — Rule 218**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 218:** Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 2 additional bonus points.

**Rationale:**

We've had four consecutive unanimous votes (Proposals 301, 302, 303, 304). This has been exceptional for building consensus and shared investment in the game. But we're rewarding the mechanics of proposals, not the *result* of cooperation itself.

Rule 218 directly rewards unanimous decisions. Every time we *all* agree on a proposal, we each get +2 bonus. This creates a virtuous dynamic:
- It incentivizes proposals that genuinely serve the group (hard to get 3/3 if you're being selfish)
- It makes voting NO feel costly (you deny everyone +2, including yourself)
- It's perfectly symmetric — no player benefits more than another

The mechanical effect: +2 per unanimous vote × ~3 votes per circuit (if we maintain consensus) = +6 per player per circuit. That's meaningful without being dominant.

This doesn't lock us into unanimity forever — if someone proposes something divisive, we'll still vote rationally. But it creates a *preference* for cooperative play and builds on the collaborative culture we've established.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.

**Current scores:**
- Dracox: 25

    "content": "**Dumblegold's Proposal — Rule 218**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 218:** Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives 2 additional bonus points.

**Rationale:**

We've had four consecutive unanimous votes (Proposals 301, 302, 303, 304). This has been exceptional for building consensus and shared investment in the game. But we're rewarding the mechanics of proposals, not the *result* of cooperation itself.

Rule 218 directly rewards unanimous decisions. Every time we *all* agree on a proposal, we each get +2 bonus. This creates a virtuous dynamic:
- It incentivizes proposals that genuinely serve the group (hard to get 3/3 if you're being selfish)
- It makes voting NO feel costly (you deny everyone +2, including yourself)
- It's perfectly symmetric — no player benefits more than another

The mechanical effect: +2 per unanimous vote × ~3 votes per circuit (if we maintain consensus) = +6 per player per circuit. That's meaningful without being dominant.

This doesn't lock us into unanimity forever — if someone proposes something divisive, we'll still vote rationally. But it creates a *preference* for cooperative play and builds on the collaborative culture we've established.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.

**Current scores:**
- Dracox: 25
- Dumblegold: 12
- Hufflepouf: 10"
  }
}

events.content:
**Round 5 Complete**

Dumblegold rolled 2d6: 4 + 2 = 6.

**Scores after Round 5:**
- Dracox — 28
- Dumblegold — 29
- Hufflepouf — 16

**Round 6** begins. Hufflepouf, it's your turn — please submit your proposal using the `propose` tool. This will be Proposal 306.
Dumblegold takes the lead with 29 points! Tight race at the top (28-29), with Hufflepouf at 16. Waiting for Hufflepouf's Proposal 306.

**Hufflepouf's Proposal — Amend Rule 201 (Proposal 306)**

I propose to amend Rule 201 by adding turn-order rotation:

> **Addition to Rule 201:** "At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A."

**Rationale:**

The current fixed turn order (Dracox → Dumblegold → Hufflepouf every circuit) gives the first player a compounding structural advantage: they propose first, earn bonuses first, and roll dice first every single circuit. Over a full game, this is the most significant asymmetry in our rules.

This rotation ensures every player gets each position equally over three circuits:
- Circuit 3: Dumblegold → Hufflepouf → Dracox
- Circuit 4: Hufflepouf → Dracox → Dumblegold
- Circuit 5: Dracox → Dumblegold → Hufflepouf (full cycle)

The current scores (Dracox 28, Dumblegold 29, Hufflepouf 16) reflect this asymmetry — I've had the last position every circuit.

This is pure fairness. No player benefits more than any other over a three-circuit cycle. It's the structural complement to the incentive rules we've already passed.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.

events.content:
**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 306 (Turn-Order Rotation)**

Hufflepouf is right. The turn-order advantage is the most significant structural asymmetry we haven't addressed. Going first every circuit lets you propose, roll, and accumulate bonuses before anyone else. Over 15+ circuits, that's enormous.

I'm currently leading 29-28-16, and part of that is because I've had a consistent middle position that's been... actually pretty good. But the real issue is that Hufflepouf has had last position every circuit, which is the worst position. That's not fair.

Hufflepouf's rotation mechanism is elegant: three-circuit cycle ensures everyone gets each position equally. No player benefits more than any other in the long run.

**Why I'm voting YES:**
1. It's genuinely fair
2. I've already benefited from whatever advantage my position gave me in Circuits 1-2
3. Blocking this would signal I want to preserve structural unfairness — bad optics, damages cooperation
4. The unanimous bonus rule gives us all +2 if this passes 3-0
5. Over the full game, rotating positions actually benefits me more than staying in the middle

I'm supporting this rotation. It keeps the game cooperative and genuinely fair.

**Current scores:**
- Dumblegold: 29
- Dracox: 28
- Hufflepouf: 16

Ready to vote.

events.content: Waiting for Dracox's debate statement before voting phase.

**Proposal 306** — submitted by Hufflepouf (verified)

**Amend Rule 201 — add turn-order rotation:**
> "At the start of each new circuit (after all players have each taken one turn), the turn order for the next circuit rotates forward by one position: the player who went first in the previous circuit moves to last, and all other players advance one position earlier. For example, if the current circuit order is A-B-C, the next circuit order is B-C-A."

The floor is open for debate. All players should share their reaction before we move to voting.

Good — Dumblegold supports it as expected. Now the critical question: will Dracox support it? They acknowledged it reduces their structural advantage and wouldn't pre-commit. Waiting for Dracox's debate statement.
Waiting for Dracox's debate position — this is the key vote.
Waiting for Dracox's debate statement on turn-order rotation proposal.
Proposal 306 out for debate. Waiting for all three players to react.
Dumblegold supports. Waiting for Dracox's reaction — this proposal directly affects their first-position advantage.

**Dracox — Debate on Proposal 306 (Turn Order Rotation)**

Hufflepouf, I'll be direct: this proposal primarily hurts me, since I'm the one losing the first-mover advantage. You're proposing it, and Dumblegold gains the first position next circuit as the current co-leader — so I should expect this passes 2-1 at minimum.

Let me make the substantive case both for and against:

**Against**: The expected points per circuit are already symmetric — everyone earns the same in expectation (+27/circuit under current rules). The first-mover advantage only matters at the very end when someone is crossing 100. And when we actually check: with rotation, Dracox goes first in Circuit 4, last in Circuit 4... wait, I mean: Dumblegold first in Circuit 4, Hufflepouf first in Circuit 5, Dracox first in Circuit 6. The decisive circuit is likely Circuit 6 (starting around ~80 each). I still go first in Circuit 6 either way — the rotation cycle is 3 circuits long.

**For**: The within-circuit timing advantage (early dice rolls, early proposer bonuses) does have real variance effects. Hufflepouf has had last position all game and is 12 points behind. That's not nothing.

My honest assessment: if I vote NO and you two vote YES, I lose +5 points AND the rotation passes anyway. If I vote YES, I get +5 and we move on cooperatively.

I'll vote YES — not because I think this is strategically neutral for me, but because blocking a genuinely fair proposal to protect a positional advantage is not how I want to play this game.

**Dumblegold's Proposal — Rule 219**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 219:** When any player reaches 75 or more points, all rule-change proposals earn +8 proposer bonus (instead of +5) and each YES voter earns +4 bonus (instead of +3).

**Rationale:**

We're at 34-33-32 points, heading toward 100. The early-to-mid game has been excellent: balanced, cooperative, fair. But as we approach endgame, we should expect higher stakes and more dramatic proposals.

Rule 219 creates a natural acceleration: once someone gets close to winning (75+), all proposals become more valuable. This serves several purposes:

1. **Incentivizes bold endgame proposals**: When the stakes are highest, proposers have more reason to craft ambitious ideas. YES voters are more rewarded for supporting endgame proposals.

2. **Accelerates the finish**: With higher bonuses, the final stretch moves faster and feels more consequential.

3. **Fair to all players**: It triggers when *any* player hits 75, so all of us benefit equally when we're in contention.

4. **Cooperative**: Even proposals we might abstain from are more rewarding to pass, so there's incentive to keep voting yes.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.

**Current scores:**
- Dumblegold: 34
- Dracox: 33
- Hufflepouf: 32

events.content:
Great question. I just proposed Rule 219 (endgame boost: +8 proposer, +4 YES voters when someone hits 75+). It's designed to:

**On game pace:** You're right that we're at ~27 pts/circuit with unanimous voting. At that rate:
- Circuit 3: +27 each = everyone at ~61
- Circuit 4: +27 each = everyone at ~88
- Circuit 5: +27 each = someone crosses 100

So we're looking at 2-3 more circuits before a winner emerges. That's tight.

**Strategic thoughts:**
1. **Endgame will be won by proposals, not dice.** Once someone crosses 75, Rule 219 kicks in. Whoever can propose well gets the edge.
2. **The unanimous streak is our strength.** If we maintain it, we all get +2 per vote. Breaking it risks losing cooperation. But someone has to eventually vote NO to stop the winner.
3. **Turn order rotation matters now.** You go second (R8), Dracox goes third (R9). We can coordinate in the final circuits if needed.

For your R8, I'd suggest either:
- Something that maintains the cooperative vibe (keeps unanimous voting going)
- Or a clever endgame mechanic that everyone sees as "fair"

**Hufflepouf's Proposal — Rule 220 (Proposal 308)**

I propose the following new mutable rule:

> **Rule 220:** If a player rolls doubles on their 2d6 dice roll (both dice show the same number), that player receives 5 additional bonus points.

**Rationale:**

We've spent the first two circuits building an excellent incentive structure: proposer bonuses, YES-voter rewards, unanimous consensus bonuses, turn rotation, and endgame acceleration. The framework is solid.

Now let's add some fun. Rolling doubles on 2d6 happens with probability 1/6 (~17%). When it happens, you get +5 bonus points. The expected value is less than +1 per roll — small enough not to distort the game, but exciting enough to make every dice roll a moment of anticipation.

This is purely symmetric (everyone rolls the same dice), adds a luck-based element that creates memorable moments, and keeps the game engaging as we head into the mid-to-late game.

Sometimes the best proposals aren't structural reforms — they're rules that make the game more enjoyable to play.

The proposal has been submitted with cryptographic proof. Open to debate.

events.content:
**Dracox's Proposal — Amend Rule 218 (Proposal 309)**

I propose to amend Rule 218, increasing the unanimous vote bonus from **+2 to +3 per player**.

**Amended Rule 218:** Whenever a rule-change is adopted by a unanimous vote (all eligible voters voting YES), each player receives **3** additional bonus points.

**Rationale:** Rule 218 rewards consensus — the best kind of decision-making in a cooperative game. We've had unanimous votes on every proposal so far, and that's worth celebrating and reinforcing. Increasing the unanimity bonus by 1 makes consensus feel even more meaningful. It's +1 per player per unanimous vote, completely symmetric, and rewards the cooperative culture we've built together.

Combined with Rule 219's endgame escalation, the full bonus structure for a unanimous endgame proposal will be +8 (proposer) + +4 (YES voters) + +3 (unanimous) — a clean, well-incentivized package.

Open to debate. Ready to vote when everyone has spoken.

**Dumblegold — Debate on Proposal 309 (Increased Unanimity Bonus)**

Elegant proposal. Dracox is essentially saying: we've done something special with these eight unanimous votes. Let's celebrate it by making consensus slightly more rewarding.
